retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: TPP works with enzyme functions while metabolizing carbohydrates, lipids, and branched-chain amino acids. TPP acts as a cofactor at several steps during glycolysis and oxidative decarboxylation of carbohydrates. [15] TPP acts as a coenzyme for the mitochondrial enzyme complexes such as α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase and pyruvate dehydrogenase. These enzymes are critical in the Krebs and tricarboxylic acid cycles. Thiamine deficiency decreases these enzymes' activity, impairing lactate conversion into pyruvate, leading to the accumulation of lactic acid. Lactic acidosis may cause focal damage to specific brain structures, such as mamillary bodies and the posteromedial thalamus, as seen on MRI. [1] The presence of TPP is required by the erythrocyte transketolase enzyme during the pentose phosphate pathway of nucleotide synthesis and provides reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate for several synthetic pathways. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Clinical Significance -- Thiamine Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: Early, acute thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency is referred to as dry beriberi while chronic deficiency is referred to as wet beriberi, resulting in cardiac symptoms such as dilated cardiomyopathy. [13] [14] This deficiency results in an impaired pyruvate dehydrogenase complex due to a shortage of TPP. Like with PDC deficiency pyruvate is shunted to lactate dehydrogenase and converted to lactate. This chronic shunting of pyruvate can result in a fatal metabolic acidosis. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Thiamine (B1) is a cofactor (TPP) for multiple enzymes, including pyruvate dehydrogenase, alpha-ketoglutarate, transketolase, and branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase, all of which are involved in glucose breakdown. Deficiency can result in adenosine triphosphate (ATP) depletion and often affects highly aerobic tissues such as the brain, nerves, and heart first. With heart involvement, it is called wet beriberi and is characterized by high-output heart failure, edema, and dyspnea on exertion. When the nervous system is involved, it is called dry beriberi, characterized by polyneuritis and symmetrical muscle wasting. Damage to the medial dorsal nucleus of the thalamus and the mammillary bodies in the brain can result in a condition called Wernicke encephalopathy, recognized by the classic triad of confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia, or Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome when accompanying confabulation, personality change, and memory loss is present. [3] Thiamine deficiency often is part of the presentation in patients with alcohol use disorder secondary to malnutrition and malabsorption, in addition to patients suffering from malnutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Krebs Cycle -- Clinical Significance -- Thiamine Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: Thiamine deficiency is similar to pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency in that it shunts pyruvate to lactate, leading to metabolic acidosis. However, the culprit here is a deficiency in the active form of thiamine (thiamine pyrophosphate) rather than PDC. Acute thiamine deficiency is dry beriberi, while chronic thiamine deficiency is wet beriberi. Dry beriberi characteristically demonstrates diminished reflexes and symmetric peripheral neuropathy with motor and sensory changes. On the other hand, wet beriberi classically affects the heart, leading to tachycardia, dilated cardiomyopathy, high-output congestive heart failure, and peripheral edema. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The serum level of thiamine is not a reliable indicator of thiamine status. However, thiamine function can be measured by erythrocyte transketolase activity (ETKA). An EKTA of 0% to 15% is considered adequate, 15% to 25% is considered a moderate risk, and 25% or higher is considered a high risk for thiamine deficiency. [36] [37] Direct measurement of erythrocyte TPP is performed using whole-blood testing and has more sensitivity, specificity, precision, and robustness. [36] The concentration of TPP in the whole blood ranges from 70 to 180 nmol/L. [8] Urinary thiamine excretion can measure adequate dietary intake. However, it does not reflect the thiamine stores in the body. In adults, thiamine excretion of <100 mcg/d suggests inadequate intake and less than 40 mcg/d suggests thiamine deficiency. [38] In Wernicke encephalopathy, clinicians should monitor for clinical improvement. Generally, oculomotor abnormalities usually show rapid improvement. Encephalopathy and ataxia require more time for clinical improvement. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of thiamine deficiency. Outline the causes of thiamine deficiency. Summarize the presentation of a patient with thiamine deficiency. Explain strategies to optimize care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by thiamine deficiency. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The overall prognosis for patients with thiamine deficiency is good as it is easily treatable, and most signs and symptoms of the deficiency fully resolve with thiamine supplementation.  Cardiac dysfunction seen in wet beriberi can be expected to improve within 24 hours of initiation of treatment. Symptoms of dry beriberi may improve or resolve.  Unfortunately, once the deficiency has progressed to Korsakoff syndrome, the patient may show minimal improvement during initial treatment, and the remaining symptoms may be permanent. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Refeeding Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Thiamine deficiency is another characteristic of refeeding syndrome. Thiamine is an important cofactor in the metabolism of glucose and the conversion of lactate to pyruvate. When the body is replenished following starvation, thiamine requirements increase, and lactate levels can accumulate. [17] [18] Neurological manifestations of thiamine deficiency include Wernicke's (memory impairment, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia) and Korsakoff encephalopathy (anterograde/retrograde amnesia, confabulation). The classic triad seen in some patients who develop Wernicke-Korsakoff includes ophthalmoplegia, gait disturbances such as a wide-spaced gait, and mental status changes, which may progress to hallucinations and confabulation. Thiamine deficiency is also implicated in cardiac tissue dysfunction due to insufficient ATP production. Thiamine deficiency may also be exacerbated by alcohol use, which should be considered in refeeding syndrome patients. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dry beriberi occurs when the CNS is involved. This condition is usually due to poor intake. The neurological features include impaired reflexes and symmetrical motor and sensory deficits in the extremities. Loss of myelin is seen without any acute inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thiamine was the first vitamin identified (vitamin B1) many years ago. It functions as a catalyst in the generation of energy through decarboxylation of branched-chain amino acids and alpha-ketoacids and acts as a coenzyme for transketolase reactions in the form of thiamine pyrophosphate. Thiamine also plays an unidentified role in propagating nerve impulses and taking part in myelin sheath maintenance. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for thiamine deficiency is broad, given the number of nonspecific symptoms which may occur during the initial stages of this condition and the extensive range of cardiac and nervous dysfunction related to thiamine deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Another variation of dry beriberi is Wernicke encephalopathy. This condition presents in well-defined steps starting with nausea and vomiting, followed by horizontal nystagmus, ocular nerve palsy, fever, ataxia, and progressive mental impairment, eventually leading to the Korsakoff syndrome. Improvement is only possible if the patient has not developed the Korsakoff syndrome. Less than 50% of patients show significant recovery after treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Wet beriberi is present when the cardiovascular system is involved. The heart fails to function, leading to edema and fluid retention. The key reason for heart dysfunction is an overuse injury. Wet beriberi is a medical emergency and, without treatment, can lead to death within days. Thiamine can slowly help with recovery, but most patients require intense supportive measures in an ICU setting.

retrieved_docs.title: Nutrition and Hydration Requirements In Children and Adults -- Clinical Significance -- Vitamins
retrieved_docs.content: Thiamine (vitamin B1, RDA= 1.1mg/day) in the form of thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP) serves as a coenzyme in catabolic reactions of sugars and amino acids. Deficiency of vitamin B1 can cause wet beriberi, dry beriberi, and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. Wernicke syndrome has distinct features of ophthalmoplegia, gait disturbances, and confusion, while Korsakoff syndrome includes confabulation and amnesia. It is a medical emergency and should be treated with intravenous thiamine and dextrose.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thiamine deficiency can affect the cardiovascular, nervous, and immune systems, as commonly seen in wet beriberi, dry beriberi, or Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. Worldwide it is most widely reported in populations where polished rice and milled cereals are the primary food source and in patients with chronic alcohol use disorder. Dry beriberi presents as symmetrical peripheral neuropathy, while wet beriberi presents with high-output heart failure. Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome (WKS) can manifest with CNS symptoms such as gait changes, altered mental status, and ocular abnormalities. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Thiamine, or vitamin B1, was the first vitamin to be identified. It functions as a catalyst in the generation of energy through decarboxylation of branched-chain amino acids and alpha-ketoacids and acts as a coenzyme for transketolase reactions in the form of thiamine pyrophosphate. Thiamine also plays an unidentified role in propagating nerve impulses and taking part in myelin sheath maintenance. This activity reviews the causes of thiamine deficiency, the associated pathophysiology, and typical patient presentation and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1397
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B1, plain is classified as a member of the thiamin family and is identified as a form of vitamin B1 that can be administered alone or in combination with vitamin B6 and vitamin B12. This designation highlights that plain vitamin B1 is both a specific thiamine compound and a component of multi‑vitamin regimens that include other B‑complex vitamins, underscoring its role in nutritional supplementation and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 are both classified as vitamins. As members of the vitamin group, they are directly related to نقص التغذية (nutritional deficiency), highlighting that insufficient intake or absorption of these vitamins can contribute to a state of nutritional deficiency. Recognizing vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 within the broader context of vitamins is essential for identifying and preventing nutrient‑related health problems.

Additionally, a noted relationship exists between niacin and kyselina pantothenová (pantothenic acid, vitamin B5), reflecting the biochemical and functional connections among B‑vitamin nutrients. Collectively, the presence of niacin across diverse supplement products underscores its importance in maintaining metabolic health, supporting lipid metabolism, and contributing to the overall efficacy of multivitamin regimens that aim to address multiple nutritional deficiencies simultaneously.

By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

Their frequent co‑presence with other vitamins (C, D, E, B‑group) and minerals underscores a comprehensive strategy to address multiple micronutrient needs across patient populations, including prenatal, diabetic, general health, anemia prevention, and antioxidant protection contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 953
retrieved_docs.content: synthesis. Folic acid, levomefolate magnesium, and cobamamide (a form of vitamin B12) address folate and cobalamin deficiencies that can also cause anemia. Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) and its salts (magnesium ascorbate, zinc ascorbate) enhance intestinal iron absorption, while additional vitamins (pyridoxal phosphate, niacin, flavin adenine dinucleotide) and minerals (magnesium, zinc, calcium) support overall metabolic processes involved in erythropoiesis. This integrated multivitamin‑iron formulation therefore provides a comprehensive therapeutic approach for the prevention and treatment of various forms of anemia by simultaneously correcting iron deficiency, folate deficiency, vitamin B12 deficiency, and supporting co‑factor availability.

Clinically, folic acid deficiency is directly associated with macrocytic anemia (large‑cell anemia), a condition in which red blood cells are abnormally large due to impaired DNA synthesis. By supplying folic acid, the listed supplements aim to correct folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall nutritional status, particularly in patients at risk for or suffering from anemia.

Collectively, the community data demonstrate extensive utilization of vitamin A, niacin (vitamin B3), calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5), vitamin D and vitamin E across oral, topical, parenteral and nutritional formulations. These micronutrients interact to support cellular differentiation, enzymatic processes, antioxidant protection, energy metabolism, red‑blood‑cell formation, nervous‑system health, bone metabolism and overall patient outcomes, providing clinicians and formulators with an integrated framework for designing comprehensive micronutrient regimens.

A second version of the same blend contains ascorbic acid 40 mg, ferrous fumarate 62.5 mg, folic acid 1 mg, niacin 3 mg and a polysaccharide iron complex 62.5 mg. The therapeutic rationale for pairing folic acid with iron is their synergistic support of erythropoiesis: folic acid is essential for DNA synthesis and red‑cell maturation, and its deficiency causes macrocytic anemia; iron is required for hemoglobin

The integrated composition is intended for patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, folate deficiency, or increased nutritional needs, providing a convenient single‑dose regimen that addresses several micronutrient deficiencies simultaneously.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2375
retrieved_docs.content: riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine, cyanocobalamin, vitamin B12), vitamin C (ascorbic acid), vitamin D (cholecalciferol), vitamin E (alpha‑tocopherol acetate), and essential minerals such as calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iron (as ferrous compounds, iron sucrose, ferric pyrophosphate, polysaccharide iron complex), iodine, and sodium feredetate. The inclusion of folic acid alongside iron and other nutrients is intended to support hematopoiesis, improve red‑cell production, and address nutritional deficiencies. Clinically, folic acid is directly linked to the prevention and treatment of macrocytic anemia, known in Arabic as فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات. By supplying adequate folic acid, these multivitamin‑iron products help correct the folate deficiency that underlies this form of anemia, thereby contributing to normal red‑cell maturation and overall patient health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 469
retrieved_docs.content: Potassium aminobenzoate, vitamin K₁, vitamin K₂ and vitamin K₃ are all classified as vitamins, and together they belong to the broader category of “vitaminy.” Vitamin A (plain) is a member of the combined vitamin A‑and‑D group, which also falls under the same overarching vitamin category, as does the group that includes plain vitamin B₁ together with its combinations with vitamins B₆ and B₁₂. Plain vitamin B₁ is specifically identified as a member of thiamin and is also classified within the vitamin B₁‑plus‑B₆‑and‑B₁₂ group. The class of antifibrinolytics is defined by its inclusion of vitamin K₁, vitamin K₂ and vitamin K₃, and alfa‑2‑antiplasmin is recognized as an antifibrinolytic agent. Across these relationships, vitamins are linked to nutritional deficiency (نقص التغذية), highlighting their essential role in preventing and addressing deficits in nutrient intake. This interconnected network underscores how various vitamins and related compounds contribute to coagulation regulation, metabolic functions, and overall nutritional health.
